title: Friday
html:
I check the planet before I put my blazer on.

---

[Planet designation: Sphere 7-A]

[Status: Barren]

[Day 12 of 12: Water processing complete.]

[Atmospheric development: 47% complete. Minimum threshold reached.]

[Terrain: Rocky. Liquid water present in low-lying regions. Expanding.]

[Purchased organisms (x40): 6 surviving. Critical.]

[Unclassified organisms: 3 variants active. Population: estimated 4,200. Reproduction rate: stable.]

[Life Energy generated: 1.83]

---

1.83. Up from 0.41 three days ago. The atmosphere crossed the minimum threshold overnight, which means the purchased organisms that made it this far have breathable air now, or the closest thing to it. The 6 survivors might stabilize. The unclassified organisms are at 4,200 and climbing.

I close the panel and finish getting dressed.

***

The property management company is on the sixth floor of a building with a working elevator and a receptionist who offers me water when I arrive. The office is cooler than it is outside. I take the water.

The interviewer is a man named Mr. Sorin who comes out to collect me himself, which is a good sign. He's in his fifties, jacket off, reading glasses pushed up on his forehead. He asks if I found the place easily before we've sat down.

I did. I say so.

He has my resume on the table between us but he doesn't look at it much. He asks what I was doing in my previous role, I walk him through it, and then he asks something the insurance woman didn't: what broke down, and how did I handle it.

I think about that for a second. There was a client last year who sent the same purchase order twice in the same week and couldn't be reached to clarify, and the report deadline was the next morning. I handled it by flagging the duplicate, sending the report with a note on both versions, and calling the client at 7 AM before the deadline. The client was annoyed about the call. The report was on time.

Mr. Sorin writes something and nods.

He tells me about the role. High call volume, walk-ins, some document handling, coordinating between tenants and the building managers. The previous person stayed four years. I don't ask why they left.

"You're overqualified," he says. Not unkindly.

"I need work," I say, which is the same thing I told the insurance woman. This time I add: "I'm not looking to move up fast. I want something stable."

He looks at me for a moment. "We'll be in touch by Monday."

That's not a rejection. I thank him for his time and take the elevator down.

***

I check email on my phone in the building lobby. Nothing from the insurance company, which means I didn't get it. No one sends a rejection within the same week unless it's automated. If it were good news it would have come faster.

I ride home and change out of the blazer. The ball of light is in the room, noticeably brighter than this morning. I don't know if it tracks the planet or tracks something else. I've stopped trying to figure out what it's reacting to.

I open the Authority.

---

[Planet designation: Sphere 7-A]

[Status: Developing]

[Atmospheric development: 49% complete.]

[Temperature range: -38 to -44 degrees Celsius. Slight warming detected.]

[Purchased organisms (x40): 6 surviving. Stabilizing.]

[Unclassified organisms: 3 variants active. Population: estimated 5,100. Reproduction rate: increasing.]

[Life Energy generated: 2.09]

---

2.09. It's been six hours and it went up 0.26. The status changed from Barren to Developing.

I sit with that number. At this rate, 2 Life Energy per day, it'll take 500 days to hit 1,000. Rent is due in 2 days. The conversion rate is 1,000 Life Energy to 3 months of rent, which means I need roughly 333 Life Energy per month, which means I need to be generating around 11 Life Energy per day before this becomes a meaningful income. I'm generating 2.

I close the panel.

The practical issue is the same as it's been. Not enough life on the planet. The unclassified organisms are doing the work but 5,000 organisms of bacteria-level complexity don't generate much. I need either more organisms or more complex ones, and both options cost Life Energy I don't have, or they cost materials I can find at home.

I look around my room. The plant pot is mostly used up. I've sent tap water four times. I've been scraping the inside of the pot for the last two days and the system keeps logging it as the same unclassified origin.

There are other sources. I think about this practically. Soil from outside. The patch of ground near the building entrance where something is growing between the paving stones, small weeds, ground that hasn't been treated. That soil hasn't been sitting in a dry pot for however long. It'll have more in it.

I go downstairs with an empty takeaway container and a spoon.

The patch near the entrance is small, maybe 30 centimeters of cracked ground between two paving stones where a plant is pushing through, pale green, thin-stemmed, the kind of thing that grows where nothing is supposed to. I scrape soil into the container, working around the roots. I take some of the plant too, a small section, stem and root attached. It's a weed. No one will notice.

Back upstairs I press the soil against the panel in batches, then the plant stem separately.

---

[External input detected. Origin: unclassified. Processing.]

[New organism variants: 7 additional variants identified. Classification pending.]

[Plant matter detected. Processing.]

[New input: Multicellular organism detected. Classification: rudimentary flora. Viability assessment in progress.]

---

Multicellular. The single-cell Shop organisms have been dying for days and they were the paid ones. A weed from the pavement might be the most useful thing I've put on that planet.

I check viability.

---

[Rudimentary flora: Viability in current conditions: low. Survival probability without atmospheric improvement: 12%. Survival probability at 60% atmospheric development: 41%.]

---

12% now. 41% at 60% atmosphere, which the planet will hit in roughly 4 more days at current development rate. If the weed survives those 4 days, it has a real chance. Plants produce oxygen. Oxygen improves atmosphere. If one plant takes hold, the growth curve changes.

I look at the plant stem. I only took one.

I go back downstairs and take three more.

***

Rent is due Monday. Today is Friday. Mr. Sorin said he'd be in touch by Monday.

I open the application list. 31 applications total. 2 standard rejections, 6 auto-rejections, 1 acknowledgement that went nowhere, 1 pending for the property company, 21 no response. I add the insurance interview to the rejection column without waiting for the email.

The savings account has enough for one month of rent. I checked this morning while bracing. After rent, the number left is not enough for the following month. It's enough for food and transport for about three weeks, at the pace I currently spend.

I find three new listings and apply to all of them. One is a junior logistics coordinator, which is essentially my old job at a lower title, lower pay, and a different company. I write a cover letter that doesn't mention that and send it.

At 6 PM the planet ticks over.

---

[Life Energy generated: 2.71]

[Rudimentary flora: 2 of 4 specimens show root establishment. Cellular activity detected.]

[Unclassified organisms: Population 6,800. Reproducing across 4 terrain zones.]

---

2 of 4 plants taking hold. The unclassified organism population jumped from 5,100 to 6,800 in a few hours, which means the new soil variants are reproducing faster than the original three. 4 terrain zones instead of just the low-lying water regions.

2.71 Life Energy. Still nowhere near what I need. But it's moving.

I eat the last of the instant noodles. I should buy food. I add that to the list under applications.

The ball of light is the brightest it's been since this started. I wave my hand through it on the way to the sink. Zap! I check my palm. Nothing visible. I stopped checking after the first week but I still look.

I wash the container from the pavement soil and put it on the drying rack. Then I open the Authority again, not to do anything, just to look at the numbers.

---

[Life Energy generated: 2.74]

---

Three more since I checked.

I close it and try to sleep.

***

Saturday. No email from the property company, which is expected because he said Monday. No email from the insurance company, which means the rejection is coming but hasn't arrived yet. I make rice and sit at the desk.

I look up what lives in urban pavement soil. This is not something I expected to be researching. The results are: bacteria, yes, fungi, nematodes in larger quantities than I expected, algae where there's moisture, sometimes mosses, sometimes seeds waiting for conditions to change. The research is framed for people who care about ecosystems. I'm trying to figure out what I accidentally sent to another planet.

The nematodes are interesting. They're small worms, microscopic, and they eat bacteria. If they reproduce on the planet they'll start consuming the bacteria population, which is the unclassified organism base I currently have. That's bad for numbers in the short term. But nematodes are more complex than bacteria, and more complex organisms generate more Life Energy per unit.

I don't know if the system works that way. But it generated 0 for tap water bacteria for days until they reproduced, then it registered. More complex things might register faster or at a higher rate.

The plants matter too. If the 2 that took root produce oxygen and that improves the atmosphere faster, the 6 purchased organisms that are still alive might recover. They've been struggling since Day 1 and they cost me 200 Life Energy.

I open the planet panel.

---

[Planet designation: Sphere 7-A]

[Status: Developing]

[Atmospheric development: 53% complete.]

[Temperature range: -36 to -42 degrees Celsius.]

[Purchased organisms (x40): 6 surviving. Stable.]

[Unclassified organisms: 11 variants active. Population: estimated 9,300.]

[Rudimentary flora: 2 established. Photosynthetic activity initiated. Atmospheric contribution: trace.]

[Life Energy generated: 3.18]

---

Photosynthetic activity. The plants are doing something. 11 variants now, up from 3. The pavement soil added 8 new types of organism.

3.18 Life Energy.

Rent is in 2 days. I need 333 Life Energy to cover one month. I'm generating 3 a day. These numbers don't work yet.

I close the panel and look at the research tab I still have open. Nematodes. Bacteria. Fungi. Moss.

I think about what else is in this building. The water tank on the roof collects things. The condensation on the pipes in the bathroom has something living in it, orange-brown streaks on the grout that I've been ignoring for two years. That's a biofilm. Biofilms are communities of microorganisms.

I look at the bathroom wall for a while.

Then I get a cloth.
